answer_length_cues.correct: Waiver approval is governed by FAA Order JO 7210.3, Chapter 19, Section 7, which is a different order and chapter from the 1-1-8 change process.

No; military operations require prior approval by the appropriate military headquarters, and for USAF procedures involving military aircraft only, HQ Air Force Flight Standards Agency is the approval authority.
The FAA Air Traffic Plans and Publications website, and the FAA Orders and Notices website.
No; 'will' means futurity, not a requirement for the application of a procedure. 'Must' is the term that means mandatory.
No — control the traffic to avoid conflicts in the area where the emergency is being handled and along routes needed by emergency equipment.
From at least 30 minutes before the ETA until the aircraft is located or for 30 minutes after fuel is estimated exhausted.
The emergency equipment crew, the airport management, and the appropriate military agencies when requested by the pilot.
All aircraft receiving ATC service, all other aircraft with a filed flight plan or otherwise known to the ATC unit, and any aircraft known or believed to be subject to unlawful interference.
The RCC determines the airspace required for SAR operations, the ACC blocks that airspace until the RCC releases it, and an International NOTAM is issued describing the affected airspace.

En route: notify air carrier company radio stations for VFR company aircraft in the vicinity and FSSs adjacent to the emergency. Terminal: relay all information to the ARTCC where the emergency exists and disseminate as a NOTAM.
Approve it if the pilot has requested it and will not operate in Class A or B. The pilot's request serves as confirmation that weather conditions are adequate.

Plan for the aircraft to maintain the last assigned altitude or MEA (whichever is higher) until 10 minutes beyond the clearance limit, then proceed at filed altitude(s) or flight level(s). Because U.S. airspace is within 10 minutes of the clearance limit (50 NM in a turbojet), the climb to border-crossing altitude will commence at the estimated time of crossing the Canada/U.S. boundary.

Use EDST for strategic planning and conflict prediction, but maintain radar separation using actual radar track data, because EDST is a strategic planning tool that predicts conflicts using current plans which may include unissued clearances.

Yes. The sector team must perform automation entries in a timely manner, and conflict probe accuracy in assigning alert notification is dependent upon entry/update of a flight's interim altitude. The aircraft being level on radar does not eliminate this requirement.

Recognize that Hold View automatically records the delay when the aircraft is cleared out of hold. Delay information must be recorded, and facility delay reporting directives must be followed regardless of whether TMU requests the data.

Both require continued use of the affected tools with recognition that certain data may be affected. For forecast wind loss, both alert AND trajectory data are affected. For connectivity loss, alert data may be affected.

No. Automated coordination normally constitutes complete coordination between ATOP sectors, but is insufficient when the aircraft is in conflict with another in the receiving sector or when facility directives or LOA specify otherwise.

Use voice to override with the full DISREGARD phraseology, restate the entire clearance, instruct the pilot to send UNABLE, complete required coordination before acknowledging any abnormal indication, and cancel the uplink only after the pilot acknowledges the superseding voice clearance.
Use the 3-mile tower observation, forward it to the weather observer, and notify the observer of the decrease below 4 miles.
GNSS-equipped with suffix /G, /L, /S, or /V and not on a random impromptu route.
The VFR-on-top expectation does not apply because it is limited to outside Class A airspace.
Route of flight including ADR/ADAR/AAR when applied.
Inform pilot, suggest ground delay; if pilot insists, inform clearance facility of intentions and VFR departure time.
'Descend via the Eagul Five Arrival, except cross Geeno at 10,000.'
Broadcast the stop request directly to the EA aircraft on the emergency frequency and notify the ARTCC of the direct broadcast as soon as possible.
No, because it is below 10,000 feet, is not a turbojet, and is not a presidential aircraft.
Instruct: IF RUNWAY/APPROACH LIGHTS NOT IN SIGHT, EXECUTE MISSED APPROACH, or climb to/maintain specified altitude and fly specified course
Inform aircraft: THIS WILL BE A SURVEILLANCE APPROACH TO (airport name) AIRPORT, RUNWAY 27, and specify MAP location
Clear the aircraft to an appropriate NAVAID/fix for an instrument approach
Solicit the aircraft approach category from the pilot, then issue the applicable circling MDA

location_scaffolded.prompt: A pilot transmits 'Center, N345AB, we've lost pressurization and need to descend, over.' The pilot never says 'Mayday' or 'Pan-Pan,' but the aircraft is now descending without a clearance and you believe an emergency or urgent situation exists. Another controller says the transmission cannot be treated as an emergency because the pilot used neither emergency word. Under 10-1-1, if 'Mayday' or 'Pan-Pan' are not used but you believe an emergency or urgent situation exists, handle it as though it were an emergency. Because of the infinite variety of emergencies, specific procedures cannot be prescribed; select the most appropriate course of action. How should this situation be handled? What should you do?

You are working an aircraft in distress and a nearby VFR aircraft offers to escort it to the nearest airport. The escort pilot states they have never flown formation and cannot see the distressed aircraft. Both aircraft are in instrument conditions. Under 10-1-4e, when considering an escort, evaluate the close formation required and give special consideration if the maneuver goes through clouds. Under 10-1-4f, do not allow aircraft to join up in formation during emergency conditions unless the pilots are familiar with and capable of formation flight, and they can communicate and have visual contact with each other. Should the escort be allowed? What should you do?

A fuel truck overturns on the airport movement area, and local fire vehicles are responding. A corporate jet requests taxi to the same ramp area, and the pilot asserts the route does not pass directly through the spill site. Under 10-1-6, when an emergency occurs on the airport proper, control other air and ground traffic to avoid conflicts in the area where the emergency is being handled. This also applies when routes are required for movement of local emergency equipment. Should the corporate jet be allowed to taxi? What should you do?
Which of the following would NOT by itself require you to consider an aircraft emergency and notify RCC or ARTCC? Which situation does NOT trigger emergency consideration under 10-2-5?
How does the information forwarded to the RCC under 10-3-3 differ from the information forwarded to the ARTCC? Choose the best answer.
How do the VFR avoidance minima for ground missile emergencies compare to the IFR/SVFR minima under 10-7-2 and 10-7-3? Choose the best answer.
A flight is holding at an en route fix. The controller uses Hold View to annotate the holding instructions. Under 13-1-7, is this sufficient by itself? Choose the best answer.

A pilot reports a drone hovering near the final approach path at 300 feet AGL. You determine this may constitute a suspicious UAS operation. SUPERVISORY NOTIFICATION Under 2-1-26, what action must you take, and which FAA orders provide the governing procedures?

You are coordinating on the interphone. You have three messages waiting: (1) a departure report for United 321, (2) a VFR flight following message for a Cessna, and (3) a handoff clearance for American 455. INTERPHONE TRANSMISSION PRIORITIES Under 2-4-10, determine the correct priority order for these three messages.

You are working en route and observing a heavy B747 at FL350 with 4 miles of separation from a following aircraft. You need to issue a traffic advisory to the following aircraft about the B747. WORDS AND PHRASES Under 2-4-14, determine whether you must use the word 'heavy' in your traffic advisory.

You need to coordinate with Jacksonville Approach, which is a military RATCF co-located with a civil approach facility in the same area. The civil facility is also named Jacksonville. FACILITY IDENTIFICATION Under 2-4-19, how should the military approach facility identify itself to avoid confusion?

You are working the tower. The surface visibility observation reports 5 miles of prevailing visibility, but from the tower cab you observe that the visibility at tower level is approximately 3 miles due to a low cloud layer that partially obscures your view. REPORTING WEATHER CONDITIONS Under 2-6-3, what actions must you take?

The barometric pressure at your facility has risen to 31.05 inches. A departing aircraft, Delta 123, is climbing and has a mandatory crossing altitude of 1,200 feet AGL before continuing higher. For departures under high barometric pressure procedures, advise pilots to set 31.00 before any mandatory or crossing altitude, or 1,500 feet AGL, whichever is lower. Under 2-7-3, what phraseology must you issue to Delta 123?

The touchdown RVR for Runway 27R is 5,500 feet. The mid-point RVR is 1,800 feet. The rollout RVR is 2,500 feet. Prevailing visibility is 3 miles. Report touchdown RVR when it is 6,000 feet or less. Report mid-point or rollout RVR when the value is below 2,000 feet and touchdown RVR is greater than the secondary value. Under 2-8-2, determine which RVR values must be reported and why.

An updated RwyCC report arrives for Runway 9: the codes have changed from 5-5-5 to 3-3-3. The ATIS is currently broadcasting the old report and the next update cycle is 15 minutes away. Several aircraft are on approach and one is holding for departure. Another controller says to just wait for the ATIS cycle since the codes are still being broadcast from the previous report. When an update to the RwyCC is provided, you must verbally issue the new codes to all aircraft until the ATIS broadcast can be updated. What should you do?

An aircraft is crossing a fix that serves as a reporting point for both an en route center and an approach control. The center has already received the position report. Under 6-1-3, may the approach control also require the pilot to report that fix? Choose the best answer.

A terminal controller clears an IFR departure to maintain VFR conditions for noise-abatement reasons, but the IFR departure route published in the facility SOP already conforms to the FAA-approved noise-abatement route. A trainee questions whether the clearance is valid under 7-1-2a2. Per 7-1-2a2 (TERMINAL), the clearance must result in noise-abatement benefits where part of the IFR departure route does not conform to an FAA-approved noise-abatement route or altitude. Is the clearance valid under 7-1-2a2? What should the controller do?

You are handling an offshore airspace operation involving emergency assistance procedures. Chapter 8 does not address emergency procedures for this specific offshore scenario. Under 8-1-2, provide ATC service in offshore airspace in accordance with Chapter 8 procedures and minima. For situations not covered by this chapter, the provisions in this Order apply. What should you do?

A pilot requests a VFR flight plan from Miami to San Juan at FL190 within the Miami Oceanic CTA. Weather is clear and it is midday. Under 8-1-3, VFR flight-plan operations in the Miami, Houston, and San Juan Oceanic CTAs are permitted only below FL 180, between sunrise and sunset, and in weather at or above 14 CFR 91.155. What should you do?
A controller uses lateral separation between two aircraft in oceanic airspace. Under 8-1-4, is this acceptable? Choose the best answer.

context_light_prompts.back: Must NOT use CPDLC to clear aircraft out of holding (route uplink lacks clearance limit). Must NOT use CPDLC for holding instructions if aircraft has climb/descend via. Cancel vertical nav portion first. Per 14-2-4(c).
Military command prerogative; not invoked indiscriminately by individual units or pilots.
May only be applied to specified military IFR operations documented in an LOA or other appropriate FAA/military document.
Separate aircraft and issue safety alerts as required.
Issue to aircraft on your frequency when proximity may diminish to less than applicable separation minima; where no minima apply, issue when proximity warrants.
When it appears pilot actions constitute a pilot deviation, notify the pilot workload permitting.
Eligible aircraft are separated vertically by 1,000 feet in RVSM airspace.
Use automation procedures over nonautomation procedures when workload, communications, and equipment capabilities permit.
First come, first served as circumstances permit, except listed priority situations and without compromising safety.
Only when prompt compliance is required to avoid development of an imminent situation; a later non-expedite altitude restatement cancels the expedite instruction.
Only when expeditious compliance is required to avoid an imminent situation.
Issue when aware an aircraft is in unsafe proximity to terrain, obstructions, or other aircraft.
Information about NAS components or flight conditions that may adversely affect air safety; report it as soon as possible to the appropriate facility or office.
Terminate interphone messages with your operating initials.
Monitor interphones and assigned radio frequencies continuously.
Full Route Clearance Necessary to the specified fix. If no fix is specified, full route clearance is required for the entire route.
DIRECT (degrees) DEGREES, (minutes) MINUTES (N/S), (degrees) DEGREES, (minutes) MINUTES (E/W).
Route segment that begins and ends with a published NAVAID, waypoint, fix, or airport.
Any altitude or flight level.
Before/upon approach clearance, before en route descent, prior to departure (terminal), and after changes.
Eight cardinal compass points and left/right base/downwind leg as appropriate.
Approach to one runway, landing on a parallel runway. Requires higher weather minima/MDA.
On initial contact with final controller, ask for communication check. Phraseology: '(call sign), (facility) FINAL CONTROLLER. HOW DO YOU HEAR ME?'
STARS may be used for: identifying aircraft with discrete beacon codes, maintaining target identity, and performing handoffs between controllers. All terminal STARS procedures apply to FUSION target.
IDENT. Or: SQUAWK (code) AND IDENT.
Paragraphs 7-6-1 (Application) and 7-6-2 (Service Availability) for additional approach control service procedures.
Paragraph 4-6-5 (Visual Holding Points) and Paragraph 7-6-5 (Holding).

location_scaffolded.back: Communications Center, Flight Standards, and Airport offices.
location_scaffolded.front: Three examples of non-ATO FAA regional office organizations listed in 1-1-13.

location_scaffolded.back: Order available on FAA website 30 days prior to effective date; FAA facility notifies service area distribution officer if not received; military facility notifies headquarters if not received.
location_scaffolded.front: 30-day rule under 1-1-7.

location_scaffolded.back: Standard: change to next minute at minute + 30 seconds. Time checks: given to the nearest quarter minute. Both in UTC.
location_scaffolded.front: ATC time convention vs. time check convention under 1-2-1m.

location_scaffolded.back: 1. Aircraft identification and type. 2. Nature of the emergency. 3. Pilot's desires.
location_scaffolded.front: What are the three minimum required information items for inflight emergencies under 10-2-1?

location_scaffolded.back: Normally do not request it. However, request the position of an aircraft that is not visually sighted or displayed on radar if the location is not given by the pilot.
location_scaffolded.front: What is the rule for requesting supplementary information from low-altitude military fighter-type aircraft under 10-2-1?

location_scaffolded.back: During the first 5 minutes of each hour, restricted to no more than three audio sweeps.
location_scaffolded.front: When is operational ground testing of ELTs authorized under 10-2-10?

location_scaffolded.back: EN ROUTE: Notify RCC directly. TERMINAL: Notify ARTCC which coordinates with RCC. Terminal never contacts RCC directly for ELT events.
location_scaffolded.front: What is the ELT notification path for en route vs terminal facilities under 10-2-10?

location_scaffolded.back: At least 100 yards if able, when it is impractical for the suspect aircraft to taxi to an isolated area or the pilot takes alternative action.
location_scaffolded.front: What spacing should other aircraft maintain from a suspect aircraft on the ground under 10-2-11?

location_scaffolded.back: Notify your supervisor or the facility air traffic manager. If general in nature, handle as 'Suspicious Activity.'
location_scaffolded.front: What is the first action when any bomb threat is received under 10-2-11?

location_scaffolded.back: Do NOT suggest what actions the pilot should take concerning a bomb. Instead, obtain aircraft type/series/model, precise location/description of the device if known, and other pertinent details, then notify the supervisor who contacts the FAA aviation explosives expert.
location_scaffolded.front: What must controllers NEVER do when a pilot needs technical assistance for a bomb threat under 10-2-11?

location_scaffolded.back: 1. Obtain aircraft identification and position, advise supervisor. 2. Relay nearest K-9 location to pilot when received. 3. If pilot diverts, obtain ETA and advise supervisor.
location_scaffolded.front: What are the three steps for handling an explosive detection K-9 team request under 10-2-12?

location_scaffolded.back: Do NOT withhold landing clearance. The pilot or parent company determines the pilot's actions.
location_scaffolded.front: What is the landing clearance rule during a real MANPADS threat under 10-2-13?

location_scaffolded.back: When the MANPADS information is broadcast via ATIS AND pilots indicate they have received the appropriate ATIS code. Both conditions must be met.
location_scaffolded.front: When may controller-to-pilot MANPADS transmissions stop under 10-2-13?

location_scaffolded.back: Time, location, altitude, color, and direction of the laser as reported by the pilot.
location_scaffolded.front: What details must terminal ATIS include for a laser event under 10-2-14?

location_scaffolded.back: Broadcast a general caution warning on all appropriate frequencies every 5 minutes for 20 minutes following the LAST report. Terminal facilities also include the event on ATIS for one hour following the last report.
location_scaffolded.front: What is the laser event broadcast requirement under 10-2-14?

location_scaffolded.back: 1. Remaining fuel vs airport distances. 2. Weather conditions. 3. Airport conditions. 4. NAVAID status. 5. Aircraft type. 6. Pilot's qualifications. 7. Vectoring or homing capability.
location_scaffolded.front: What are the seven factors to consider when recommending an emergency airport under 10-2-15?

location_scaffolded.back: 1. Radar. 2. Following another aircraft. 3. NAVAIDs. 4. Pilotage by landmarks. 5. Compass headings.
location_scaffolded.front: What five methods may be used to guide an aircraft to an emergency airport under 10-2-16?

location_scaffolded.back: Only when: 1. The pilot has declared an emergency, OR 2. The controller has determined an emergency exists or is imminent because the pilot cannot maintain an appropriate terrain/obstacle clearance minimum altitude.
location_scaffolded.front: When may the EOVM/EAM be used under 10-2-17?

location_scaffolded.back: 1. Consider the aircraft in an emergency situation. 2. Do NOT initiate climb clearances to turbine-powered aircraft until the aircraft exits the ash cloud. 3. Do NOT attempt escape vectors without pilot concurrence.
location_scaffolded.front: What are the three mandatory actions when an aircraft enters volcanic ash and indicates distress under 10-2-18?

location_scaffolded.back: Relay all available information to pilots so they are aware of the ash cloud's position and altitude(s), and suggest appropriate reroutes to avoid the area.
location_scaffolded.front: What should ATC do if volcanic ash is known or forecast to be present under 10-2-18?

malformed_reverse.front: Exercise best judgment when a situation is not covered by the order.

malformed_reverse.back: The schedule to which JO 7110.65 and its changes are published; listed in TBL 1-1-1.

malformed_reverse.front: Above 12,500 feet ASL or MEA (whichever higher) up to but not including 18,000 feet ASL. Only IFR and CVFR flights. IFR procedures applied to CVFR.
Base of all controlled high level airspace up to and including FL 600. Only IFR flights permitted.
Uncontrolled airspace where ATC has neither the authority nor responsibility for exercising control over air traffic.

malformed_reverse.back: Pilot's weather responsibility for the 1,000-feet-on-top clearance in Canada.
malformed_reverse.front: Pilot must ensure at least 1,000 feet above all cloud, haze, smoke, or other formation, with 3 miles or more flight visibility. Pilot's request = confirmation conditions are adequate.

malformed_reverse.back: No. The separation minimum applies regardless of the weather conditions. CVFR aircraft receive IFR-equivalent separation treatment.
malformed_reverse.front: Does good weather eliminate the separation requirement between IFR and CVFR aircraft in Canadian airspace?

malformed_reverse.back: Canadian parachute jumping rule, per 12-1-6 and its note.
malformed_reverse.front: Written authority from the Ministry of Transport is required before ATC may authorize parachute jumping.

malformed_reverse.back: Canadian SVFR weather minimum for aircraft other than helicopters.
malformed_reverse.front: 1 mile flight visibility (ground visibility when reported) for SVFR.

malformed_reverse.back: Canadian SVFR weather minimum for helicopters.
malformed_reverse.front: 1/2 mile flight visibility (ground visibility when available) for SVFR.

malformed_reverse.back: 5-3-1. Exceptions: 5-5-1 subparagraphs b2, b3 and 8-5-5 Radar Identification Application.
malformed_reverse.front: Establish and maintain radar identification of the aircraft involved before providing radar service.

malformed_reverse.back: 7-1-1: Class A airspace restrictions — three prohibitions.
malformed_reverse.front: Do not apply visual separation or issue VFR or VFR-on-top clearances in Class A airspace.

malformed_reverse.back: 8-1-1: Oceanic ATC service default and exception rule.
malformed_reverse.front: Chapter 8 procedures apply unless a directive or letter of agreement prescribes different procedures or minima.
Use Chapter 8 procedures and minima first. For situations not covered by Chapter 8, apply the provisions in the rest of the Order.

malformed_reverse.back: 8-1-4: Types of separation in oceanic/offshore procedures.
malformed_reverse.front: Vertical, horizontal (longitudinal/lateral), or radar (where coverage adequate). At least one required.
29.92 inches Hg standard, flight levels, unless directed/charted otherwise

malformed_reverse.back: Three criteria that make a flight inspection operation require special handling, per 9-1-1 Note 2.
malformed_reverse.front: Special participation of ground personnel, specific communications, or radar operation capabilities trigger special handling (9-1-1).

malformed_reverse.back: Route orbital range and altitude range for preplanned automatic flight checks per 9-1-3.
malformed_reverse.front: 6 NM to 40 NM; 1,000 feet above antenna to MEA.

malformed_reverse.back: 9-2-11 SECNOT action when information about the subject aircraft is not known.
malformed_reverse.front: Broadcast call sign on all frequencies and advise OS/CIC of response.

overloaded_backs.front: What are the seven factors to consider when recommending an emergency airport under 10-2-15?

overloaded_backs.back: 1. Call sign. 2. Number of suspected illness cases. 3. Nature of illness/risk if known. 4. Number of persons on board. 5. Number of deaths if applicable. 6. Pilot's intent. 7. Any request for assistance.
overloaded_backs.front: What seven items must be obtained when a death, illness, or public health risk is reported on board under 10-2-19?

overloaded_backs.back: 1. Phase (INCERFA/ALERFA/DETRESFA). 2. Agency/person originating. 3. Nature of emergency. 4. Significant flight plan info. 5. Last radio contact unit/time/freq. 6. Last position report. 7. Color/distinctive marks. 8. Action taken. 9. Other remarks.
overloaded_backs.front: What nine items must INCERFA/ALERFA/DETRESFA messages include under 10-6-3g?

overloaded_backs.back: 1. MEARTS-based radar surveillance. 2. Conflict Prediction and Reporting. 3. ADS-B. 4. ADS-C. 5. CPDLC. 6. AIDC. 7. Weather Deviation Tool. 8. Additional Decision Support Tools (situational awareness). 9. Electronic Flight Data including Electronic Flight Strips. Per 13-2-1(b).
1. Aircraft ID & type, 2. Departure point, 3. Original destination, 4. Position & time, 5. New destination, 6. ETA, 7. Remarks including change in fuel exhaustion time, 8. Revised ETA.
overloaded_backs.front: Under 2-2-12, list the eight items to forward when a military aircraft changes destination.

overloaded_backs.back: Block 1: ACID. Block 2: CID. Block 3: number/type/equip. Block 4: CID if req'd. Block 5: beacon code. Block 6: coord fix. Block 7: overflight coord indicator (FDIO only). Block 8: ETA at coord fix. Block 8A: RA events (required when no voice recorder). Block 9: altitude/route. Blocks 9A-9C: optional. Blocks 10-18: per facility directive.
overloaded_backs.front: Under 2-3-4, list the numbered blocks of the terminal flight progress strip.

overloaded_backs.back: a. VOR/VORTAC/TACAN airways — 'Victor' + number (low) or 'J' + number (jet). b. Alternate airways — 'Victor' + number + direction (e.g., Victor Twelve South). c. Colored/L/MF — color + number (e.g., Blue Eighty-One). d. Named routes — 'North American Route'/'Bahama Route' + number. e. ATS routes — phonetic letter(s) + number in group form. f. MTRs — 'I-R'/'V-R' + number. g. RNAV — high: 'Q' + number; low: phonetic letter + number.
overloaded_backs.front: Under 2-5-1, how are the seven route types described?

overloaded_backs.back: 1. VOR/VORTAC/TACAN/GPS Waypoint radial — name + digits of radial + 'radial' (omit 'degrees'). 2. Arc — distance + 'mile arc' + compass direction + 'of' + NAVAID name. 3. Quadrant — NE/SE/SW/NW + distance in miles from NAVAID. 4. NDB — course/bearing + 'course to'/'bearing from' + name + 'radio beacon'. 5. MLS — azimuth + 'azimuth to'/'azimuth from' + name + 'MLS'. 6. NRS waypoint — FIR letter + subset letter + latitude increment + longitude increment.
overloaded_backs.front: Under 2-5-2, what are the six methods for describing NAVAID-based positions?

overloaded_backs.back: 1) Construction on/near movement area. 2) Rough movement area portions. 3) Braking conditions (ice, snow, slush, water). 4) Snowdrifts/piles and plowed extent. 5) Parked aircraft on movement area. 6) Irregular airport lighting. 7) Volcanic ash (wet/dry if known). 8) Other pertinent conditions.
overloaded_backs.front: List the eight categories of airport condition information in 3-3-3.

overloaded_backs.back: 1) Issue latest braking report to each arriving and departing aircraft (prefer same weight class for super/heavy). 2) If no report exists for the runway, issue NO BRAKING ACTION REPORTS advisory. 3) Advise the Airport Operator of degraded braking reports (good to medium through nil). 4) Solicit PIREPs of runway braking action.
overloaded_backs.front: What four actions must be taken during Braking Action Advisories?

overloaded_backs.back: 1. IFR cancellation outside Class B/C/TRSA/basic-radar areas. 2. Aircraft on instrument/visual/contact approach has landed or changed to advisory frequency. 3. (NOT automatic — see exception at airport with < 1/2 mile radar coverage.) 4. TERMINAL VFR to Class B/C/TRSA/basic-radar airport has landed, or to other airports changed to tower/advisory. 5. TERMINAL aircraft completes radar approach.
overloaded_backs.front: 5-1-9: Five situations where radar service is AUTOMATICALLY terminated (no advisory needed)

overloaded_backs.back: Instruct aircraft if runway not in sight to execute missed approach (or climb/maintain altitude and fly specified course) when: (a) safety limits exceeded or radical target deviations, (b) position/ID in doubt, (c) radar contact lost or malfunction suspected, (d) airport conditions/traffic preclude completion. Subparagraphs a-c do NOT apply after PAR decision height.
overloaded_backs.front: Final approach abnormalities requiring missed approach (5-10-14)

overloaded_backs.back: (a) Inform all positions before terminating/reinstating; transfer/terminate flight data. (b) Inform interfaced facilities of shutdowns. (c) Initiate track/tag on all aircraft; use automated handoff. (d) Assigned altitude must be current; use climb/descent arrows. (e) FUSION mode: display assigned/pilot altitude when level. (f) Auto altitude readout may be used across positions with prearranged coordination.

1. Adequate radar performance with primary/ASR-9 targets displayed. 2. Flight data shows non-identified aircraft can give adequate return. 3. Airspace not less than: 6 NM (<40 NM from antenna), 10 NM (>=40 NM or narrowband). 4. Radar separation maintained between identified and all observed targets until nonradar separation. 5. If same heading, vector identified aircraft to avoid superimposed targets.
overloaded_backs.front: Five conditions for radar separation between identified and non-identified aircraft (5-5-1b3)

overloaded_backs.back: a. One ATC unit controls at a time. b. Transfer at estimated control boundary or agreed time/point. c. Transferring unit forwards changed flight plan/control data. d. Accepting unit notifies if unable to accept; specifies required conditions. e. Accepting unit must not alter clearance before transfer point without prior approval. f. Nonradar: communications transfer 5 min before boundary unless otherwise agreed.
overloaded_backs.front: 8-2-2: Six rules for transfer of control in oceanic ops

overloaded_backs.back: 1) ID/type. 2) Last position/altitude. 3) Direction/speed. 4) ETA over boundary. 5) Other pertinent info. 6) Radar point-out if in contact. 7) Tracking location name/phone.
overloaded_backs.front: 9-6-1: Seven items to forward when transferring balloon flight following

thin_backs.front: What must a controller do when a situation is not covered by this order?
What publication cycle are order changes synchronized with?
What year did the U.S. and Canada establish their cross-border ATC procedures agreement?
Who must be notified of a possible human trafficking report per 2-1-31?
Under 2-10-1, what is the en route facility term for 'D-Side' or 'Manual Controller'?
What two words are used to interrupt lower-priority interphone messages under 2-4-11?
Under 2-7-1, what term do USAF/USA use for estimated altimeter settings?
How long must wind shear/microburst information remain on the ATIS after the last report or indication?
What table contains the meanings of all ATC light signals?
What table controls high intensity runway, centerline, and touchdown zone lights?
What table controls HIRL associated with MALSR?
What table controls MIRL operation?
What table controls PAPI intensity settings?
What table controls ALS intensity settings?
Random RNAV nonradar: max distance between points
Military degree-distance: max distance between fixes FL 180 and above
Military degree-distance: max distance between fixes below FL 180
IFR vertical separation: FL 290+ non-RVSM vs all
IFR vertical separation: above FL 410 (general)
IFR vertical separation: above FL 600 military vs military
